questions.question: Why must an async setup component be wrapped in <Suspense> in a test?
options: Vue Test Utils requires Suspense for all async operations
Without a Suspense boundary, Vue warns and the component never fully renders
Suspense provides the mock context needed for async setup to function
The test environment doesn't support async components without Suspense

questions.question: What does flushPromises() do in the context of testing async setup components?
options: It resets all mocked fetch calls to their original implementations
It waits for all pending microtasks (Promise resolutions) to complete, including the async setup
It forces Vue to synchronously re-render the component tree
It clears the component's internal promise queue

questions.question: How should error states be captured when testing a Suspense-wrapped async component?
options: Wrap the assertion in a try/catch block
Use onErrorCaptured in the Suspense wrapper component to collect thrown errors
